msDesc.head: Chronicle of London with civic and guild documents; England (London), late 15th century

msItem.title: Form for proceding in a plea of mistreatment of an apprentice
msItem.rubric: Yff so be apprentes be mysse entreated or onlawfully chasted by his master
msItem.incipit: Vpon the which compleynt the chamberleyn shall call a seriaunt of the chambre and comaund hym to somon to appere before the seid chamberleyn vj oneste persones of the next aioynaunt to appere afore the seid cort by a day lymeted by the seid chamberleyn
msItem.explicit: The secund þat he is nott informed nor tawth in his occupacion. Also that he hath vnlawfull watche. Also that he hath nott his meate & drynke at due tymes.
msItem.note: Perhaps breaks off unfinished. Added afterwards: 'Also ye shall truly enquere of all suche thynges as ye shalbe charged opon so'. An addition: fol. 3 is an original parchment endleaf, with the recto blank.

msItem.title: Notes of six obits in London at which the city chamberlain receives bequests.
msItem.incipit: The secund day of October the obite of John Carpenter holden at Geldhall chapell at which obite the chamberleyn is bequest vj s. viij d.
msItem.explicit: The xix day of August the obite of Water Nele holden at seynt Jemes in Garlykhyth at whiche obite the chamberleyn is bequest iij s. iiij d.
msItem.note: 2 Oct., John Carpenter; 2 Nov., Henry Barton; 4 Nov., John Maldon grocer; Monday after Palm Sunday, William Olyvere; Friday in Whitsun week, John Reynwell; 19 August, Walter Nele.

msItem.title: Chronicle of London for 1-10 Henry IV
msItem.rubric: Nomina maiorum et vicecomitum tempore regis Henrici Quarti ...
msItem.note: Discussed Mary-Rose McLaren, The London Chronicles of the Fifteenth Century: A Revolution in English Writing (D. S. Brewer, 2002), pp. 131-132. The only event noted in addition to the succession of officers is the battle of Shrewsbury: 'The same yere was the batell of Shrewysbery on Mary Magdeleyns even'.

msItem.title: Lists of civic ordinances, almost all from London Letter Books G, H, I, K, L
msItem.incipit: Ordynaunces for weying from the comon beame, folio CCiiijxx xviij. Ordynaunces of pynnars, fol. xlviij. Ordynaunces of fullers, fol. cxvj. Ordynaunces of fullers, fol. ccxlij.
msItem.explicit: [ordenaunces in the booke off .L.] Ordenaunc' of haberdasshers & hurers in the joyernall when maister Hampton was maier the xxiiij day of Septembre. Ordenaunce for castyng donge in the Thamyse, folio lxxxv.
msItem.note: The latest ordinance is dated 1481. Rest of fol. 8r blank. Fols. 8v-9v ruled, blank; some text has been erased on fol. 9v.

msItem.title: Notes of ordinances made in 13 Edw. IV (1474)
msItem.incipit: The xj day of Ianyvere anno Edwardi iiijti xiij Mathew Philip maier graunted an ordynaunce that all the ledder shulde be solde at Leden Hall. The vijth day of Septembre master Verney meyer made an ordenaunce that euery man that brought ledder to leden hall shulde paie for a dyker j d.
msItem.note: Given in full. Rest of fol. 10r blank.

msItem.title: London civic and other oaths
msItem.note: Fols. 13r-19r are oaths of the Goldsmiths' Company and occur in a different order in the company's Book of Ordinances begun in 1478 by Miles Adys (for whom see Provenance) and others; printed in modern English translation in T. F. Reddaway and L. E. M. Walker, The early history of the Goldsmiths' Company, 1327-1509 (1975), pp. 212-221.

msItem.rubric: Thys ys the othe of the chamberleyn of London
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shall well and laufully serue the Cite of London in the office of chamburleyn, and all that ye haue in warde touchyng the cite, safely ye shall kepe. And the counsell of the cite ye shall nat discouer
msItem.explicit: And in all other thynges to youre office perteynyng, well and laufully ye shall haue yow. As god you helpe and all seyntes and by the boke.

msItem.rubric: Thys ys the othe of fremen of London
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shall be good and trewe vnto oure lyege lord kyng of Englond, and to hys heyres kynges, obeysaunt and obedient vnto the meyre and to the mynystres of the cite, the fraunchises and the customes therof ye shall meynteyne
msItem.explicit: so helpe yow god and holydom and by thys boke. I admyt yow into the lyberte of the cite of London as a freman. God yeue yow ioy therof.

listBibl.bibl: Pr. (from Mercers' wardens accounts), Blades, Life and Typography of Caxton, I, 86

msItem.incipit: Maistyrs, here ys A. B. desyreth to be admyttyd into the liberte of thys cyte by hys byrthe. Ye wyll witnes here to the court that the sayd A. B. ys the son of C. D. and fre born wythyn the liberte of thys cyte, thys ye wyll wytnes. And also that the same A. B. neuyr offendyd syth he was made fre whereby he shuld lese hys liberte by, to your vnderstandyng and knowlege.

Maistyr' wardeyns, here ys A. B. desyreth to be admyttyd into the liberte of thys cyte. Ye wyll witnes here to the court that the sayd A. B. ys an able man, and a man of good name and fame for to be of youre felyshyp. And that hys commyng in ys nat to deceue the kyng of hys custom, ne noon other felyshyp of thys cyte, but all suche thyngys as shall be leyde to hys charge in tyme commyng, as another freman doth accordyng to the custom of thys cite he shall do.

Masteris wardeyns here is A. B. citezein of London before this tyme was admitted into the liberte of the citee into the craft of C. D. Now the same A. B. desirith to be translate from the sayde craft of C. D. in to the felyship of E. F. ye well witnesse.

msItem.rubric: This ys the othe of the wardens of mercers
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shalbe trew vnto oure liege lord the kyng and to his eyres kynges. Ye shall also wele & truly obserue & kepe all the goode and polityke rules and ordynaunces yn & by the feliship of the mercery of olde ordeyned & made.
msItem.explicit: And all the poyntes & premisses afore rehersed ye shall wele & truly kepe to youre power. So help you god & holydom and by this boke.

msItem.rubric: The othe of the wardens off goldsmythes
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere yat ye shall be feithfull & trew to our lord the kyng and to his heyres kynges, and good & trewe assay make of gold & siluer and no thyng of gold and siluer touche but it be good & lefull.
msItem.explicit: ne no man resceiue in to the companye withoute assent of the companye, so god you helpe and all seyntes and by the boke.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shall be feithfull and trew to our lord the kyng and to his heyres kynges and the peas of this citee well kepe to your power. And good & trewe gold & siluer withoute deceite ye shall werke and do werke, and no glasses nor counterfete stones sett in gold.
msItem.explicit: & of noo bonde men, all which premysses aforeseid ye shall kepe in your behalff, so helpe you god & all seyntes and by the boke.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull and trewe to our lord the kyng and to his heyres kynges, and well and truly gader or do to be gadered the rent appertenyng to the company, and ouersee all the reparacions, vacacions and empcions of the same rentys when & as ye be commaunded by your wardens, and all the rente that ye shall gader or do to be gadered with the reparacions, vacacions and empcions that shal be in your tyme, ye shall therof trewe accomptz make & yelde when and as ye be commaunded by your wardens. So god you helpe and all seyntes and by the boke.

msItem.rubric: The othe of the clerke of the halle
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull & trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and that ye shal be good and trewe to the company and good and trewe counseill geve in tho thynges touchyng the nedes of the company after your cunnyng & power
msItem.explicit: and truly entre tho thynges þat arne to entre touchyng þe company, and the profite off the companye ye shall do in as moche as ye may. So god you help &c.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull & trewe to our liege lord the kyng and to his heyres kynges, and good and trewe serche & assay make of gold & siluer appertenyng to your crafte, sparyng no man for loue nor fauour nor no man grevyng for hate or ivell will
msItem.explicit: And ye shall redely come to the somons of the wardens of goldsmythes when ye are warned iff ye ne haue resonable excusacion, so god you helpe and alle seyntes and by the booke.

msItem.rubric: The othe of the collectour of the lyvelode
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere þat ye shall be feithfull and trewe to our liege lord the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and that by all the tyme of your occupyeng ye shall well and truly gader the rent perteynyng to the company and ouersee all the tenementz and rentes pertynyng to the seid company
msItem.explicit: and do the most profite ye can in byeng of all maner stuff and letyng of houses and tenementes longyng to the company, so god you helpe & all seyntes and by the boke.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shall be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and yee shall serue the company well & truly for the tyme that ye be wytholde, and ye shall be redy atte warnyng of the brocours to come & go wyth the wardens to all obitz and dirigees, and euery weke ones sey placebo & dirigee for all the soules that haue been of the companye, so god you helpe and all seyntes and by the boke.

Ye shall swere þat ye shall be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, & trewe ye shal be in the office of comon assayer for the crafte of goldsmythes, and ye shall trewe assayes make of all such gold & siluer as shal be brought vnto you to assaye
msItem.explicit: for the feleship & euery membre therof ye shall do it iustly & truly to your power wythoute any deceite, so god you helpe and all seyntys and by the boke.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere þat ye shall be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to heyres kynges, and that ye shal be trewe and obedyent to your wardeins and of good beryng to the company, and ye shall do all suche thynges as the wardeins commaunde you to do for the nedes of the company
msItem.explicit: And all other goodys of the company comyng to your handys ye shall truly kepe & deliuere to the wardeyns, and no profitz take to your selff, oute take that that is to you assigned by the wardeins. Soo god you helpe &c.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and that yee shal be trewe to your wardeins and to all the companye, and to no man selle no thyng of golde ne siluer, stone ne perle, but to fremen of the same crafte
msItem.explicit: nor no profitz take to yourselff other wise than is assigned you by the wardens & feleship. So god you &c.

msItem.rubric: The othe of the goldsmyth that shall vse the crafte of fynyng
msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shall be feithfull & trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and all siluer that ye deliuere & selle into the feleship, it shall mowe bere peny weight of allay, that it may be as good and accordyng to the kynges standard in fynesse.
msItem.explicit: wythoute any availe or profitt therof takyng to your selff, oute take that the owner shall yeve you for suche wasshyng, fynyng or partyng. So god you helpe &c.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to his heyres kynges, and ye shall no werke of gold & siluer turne & clense in no placys but there as ye shal be assigned by the wardeins
msItem.explicit: outee take that the owner shall yeve you by couenaunte for such turnyng & clensyng. So god you helpe and all seyntys & by the boke.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be feithfull and trewe to the kyng & to his eyres kynges, and ye shall be trew & obedient to your master as long as ye be hered with hym
msItem.explicit: and no profit take to your self out take is yeven you or your wages to you assigned so &c.
msItem.note: No heading: the oath of a servant (see the following item); contemporary addition in a less formal hand. Not in the Goldsmiths' Book of Ordinances.

msItem.incipit: Ye shall swere that ye shal be obedient feithfull and trew to your master, as long as ye be hered wyth hym, and ye shall do all suche thyng as a seruaunt ought to doo, to the most profyte of your master
msItem.explicit: out take your wagis or rewarde that is yeven or assigned yow, so god you helpe and all seyntys, and by the booke.
msItem.note: Later addition (s. xvi) in a less formal hand. Not in the Goldsmiths' Book of Ordinances.

msItem.title: Chronicle of London ending incomplete in 1470
msItem.incipit: The coronacion of kyng Richard the first was the iijde day of Septembre the yere of oure lord MlClxxxix. Heere follewen the names of all those persones that hath been custofes and baylyfs of the Cyte of London in the tyme off kyng Richard the first and kyng John that is to say: Henry Cornhyll, Richard Reyner - anno jo. The same day of coronacion all þe iues that were founde or myȝt be take were distroyed with wepyn or fire bothe by day and by nyght.
msItem.explicit: Richard Lee maior - Richard Gardener, Robert Droope - anno ixo. This yere after alhaloutide there was proclamacions made in London by þe kyngis commaundement that the kyng had pardoned all the northyn men for their rysyng and at þat tyme the Erle of Warwik was at Warwik and had reysed moche people

listBibl.bibl: For discussion of the text see Mary-Rose McLaren, ‘The Textual Transmission of the London Chronicles’, English Manuscript Studies 1100-1700, 3 (1992), 38-72 at 50-52; ead., The London Chronicles of the Fifteenth Century, pp. 113-115 and 253-255.
Six Town Chronicles of England, ed. R. Flenley (1911), pp. 153-164, prints extracts from 1459-1470.

msItem.title: Added entries for 1495
msItem.incipit: Also on Fryday the xxxti day off Ianyvere the seid yere satt in the seid Guyhalde on a determyn' the maier, the duke off Bukkynggam, the lorde Markes, the erle off Aroundell, the erle off Derby, the erle of Suffolke, the erle off Essex, the erle of Surrey, the erle off Vrmond
msItem.explicit: Also the xvj day off Feueryere Moneday was Sir William Stanley lorde Chamberleyne pardoned off the kyng off hangyng & drawyng and the seid day bytwene xj & xij at noone was he ledde from the towre of London to the towre hyll & there his hed smytten off and is beryed at Saint Donnstones in th
msItem.note: Breaks off. Followed by a cancelled entry for 16 May 1550

supportDesc.support: Parchment of good quality. Irregular edge on the front endleaf, fol. 3, made good with a scarf repair before the text on fol. 3v was written.
supportDesc.extent:
ii (paper) + 1 (parchment) + 49 + 1 (parchment) + ii (paper). Paper endleaves of the date of the binding.
c. 245 c. 185
supportDesc.collation: one original endleaf (fol. 3), the stub wrapped around the first quire; 1(8-1) (second leaf missing, torn out (?), after fol. 4) (fols. 4-10), 2(8)-6(8) (fols. 11-50), 7(2) (fols. 51-2, a bifolium), one original endleaf (fol. 53). No catchwords or leaf signatures visible.

layoutDesc.layout: The main part of the manuscript, fols. 4r-50v, uniformly ruled in ink for two columns of 24 lines, with single vertical and double horizontal bounding lines the full width and height of the page, no ruling between columns, and double horizontal lines in the upper margin for running heads. Fol. 16r: (18 + [6] + 14 + [151] + 52) × (23 + [60 + 11 + 60] + 30). On fols. 10v-19r the text (for which the layout seems to have been designed) comprises headings in the upper margin and text in the central ruled space, so the written area is c. 155 c. 135 .

Elsewhere in the manuscript, notably in the chronicle section, the lines in the upper margin are used to contain the first line of the text, giving an overall written area of c. 170 c. 135 , and a total of usually 27 lines, with the division into columns generally ignored, except where it is used to structure the lists of mayors and bailffs, with mayors in the left column and bailiffs in the right.

handDesc.handNote: Fols. 5r-10r, one hand, secretary with anglicana /a/.
Fols. 10v-19r: several formal cursive hands, predominantly anglicana
The chronicle, fols. 19v-50v, in one hand, predominantly secretary in aspect but with anglicana /a/.

decoDesc.decoNote: 2- to 3-line initials in gold, blue and rose on fols. 10v-12v; lettering in gold on fols. 11v-12v. The initials on fols. 10ra-12ra are attributed to the artist of MS. Douce 322 by Holly James-Maddocks, 'Scribes and booklets: the 'Trinity Anthologies' reconsidered', in Scribal Cultures in Late Medieval England: Essays in Honour of Linne R. Mooney, ed. Margaret Connolly, Holly James-Maddocks, and Derek Pearsall (Boydell & Brewer, 2022), pp. 146-179 at p. 178.
Blue initials flourished in red, fol. 11r-v.
Spaces left for decorated initials on fols. 13r-18v, not filled in.
Red paraphs and underlining of proper names in the chronicle, and initials touched in red, otherwise no decoration.

binding.p: 18th-century calf with gilt tooling; gilt decoration on the spine, with 'CVSTOMES OF LONDON' and '.T. 5:5:'
Wormholes indicate a previous binding in wooden boards.

history.provenance: Erased inscription (?), fol. 3r; other erasures, fols. 9v, 49v-50r (?).

The contents of fols. 4r-19r suggest that the volume was compiled for a chamberlain of London who was also a goldsmith. Flenley's suggestion of Miles Adys (d. c. 1497) has been elaborated by Caroline Barron ('What did medieval London merchants read?', in Medieval merchants and money. Essays in honour of James L. Bolton, ed. Martin Allen and Matthew Davies (London, 2016), pp. 43-70 at 53-4). Adys was warden of the Goldsmith's Company 1477-8, chamberlain of London 1479-1484, and controller of the Goldsmith's Company in 1492.
Algernon Capel, earl of Essex; his armorial bookplate, dated 1701.
history.acquisition: Bequeathed by him to the Bodleian.

recordHist.source: Description by Matthew Holford (Febrary 2026). Previously described in the Summary Catalogue. Summary Catalogue, vol. 4, p. 240

listBibl.bibl: Six Town Chronicles, ed. Ralph Flenley (1911), pp. 74-82

revisionDesc.change: James Cummings Up-converted the markup using https://github.com/jamescummings/Bodleian-msDesc-ODD/blob/master/convertTolkien2Bodley.xsl
